question:
There is an isometric image of a scene. In this image, the red arrow indicates the North direction.
Identify the southernmost object in the image.

Now imagine that the camera rotates COUNTER-CLOCKWISE by 90 degrees around the scene center.
When the camera rotates, the cardinal directions (north, east, south, west) rotate together with the view.

After this rotation, where will that object be located relative to the scene center in the rotated coordinate frame?

Choose one option.

Isometric image: <image>
A. east
B. northwest
C. south
D. northeast

There are two images: an isometric image and a top-view image.
- In the top-view image, top is North, bottom is South, left is West, and right is East.
- In the isometric image, the red arrow indicates the same North direction as the top-view image.
- Object directions are determined by comparing object center points (bounding box centers).

- In the isometric image, the red arrow indicates the same North direction as the top-view image.
- Object directions are determined by comparing object center points (bounding box centers).

Consider the northernmost object in the isometric image. Relative to the scene center, which direction is that object located in the top-view image? Choose one option.

Top-view image: <image>

There is an isometric image of a scene. In this image, the red arrow indicates the North direction.
Identify the westernmost object in the image.

Now imagine that the camera rotates CLOCKWISE by 90 degrees around the scene center.
When the camera rotates, the cardinal directions (north, east, south, west) rotate together with the view.

After this rotation, where will that object be located relative to the scene center in the rotated coordinate frame?

Choose one option.

A. northeast
B. southeast
C. north
